title: Switching Decision-Variable Registry
role: Authoritative vocabulary for the Networking Equipment Selector. The registry enforces MEANING (what can be constrained, how, and which values are legal). It is the SOURCE; the KB schema is a projection of it. Nothing is constrainable unless declared here. v1.0.0 unifies the former axes + config_variables split into ONE flat list of DECISION VARIABLES over configurations: a solve answers with configurations (model + uplink option + PSU set + license SKUs + cables), and whether a variable filters models or refines the kitlist is declared per variable (`eliminates`), not a structural class. validate-kb.mjs checks model values and bindings against this file.
consumers: Web UI builds form controls and panel layout from this registry (KIND drives the control; presentation drives WHERE it surfaces).
MCP server (Stage 2) builds tool parameters from this registry.
Reasoning agent (Stage 3) reasons over this registry (via the MCP server); `default.kind = must_resolve` marks the variables it must settle with the user before a BOM is final.
The KB JSON schema projects typed fields from the model-dimension variables.
change_policy: Adding a switch model is CHEAP (more data the solver already handles). Adding or changing a VARIABLE is EXPENSIVE and STRUCTURAL: it touches the UI, the MCP parameters, possibly a new solver condition type. An agent populating models must NEVER add, rename, or re-type a variable on its own — surface it for human ruling. Bump registry_version when the variable set changes.

contract._comment: The query/response contract every front-end (UI, MCP server, agent) renders. A QUERY is a list of constraints {variable, condition, value, severity(hard|soft)}; port demands use {variable:'port_count', where:{role?,medium?,speed}, ...}. The selector is STATELESS and never asks questions — it answers with resolved defaults plus honest residual choice, and dialogue is the caller's job.

response.candidates: Surviving configurations, ranked. Each carries the model plus a resolved `bom` (default SKU choices, each with a `reason`) and the per-candidate domains of its configuration variables (uplink options, PSU matrix, license groups/terms, cables).
response.open_variables: Every variable the query left unconstrained (or partially bounded), with its REMAINING domain across the survivors, its default (if any), and `must_resolve` where no safe default exists. This is the caller's 'what is still open / what should I ask next' — and the UI's facet source.
response.eliminated: Models removed by the query, each with the first violated constraint as reason.

contract.defaults: A variable without a `default` field is a pure requirement: leaving it open means 'don't care'. A `default` of kind fixed/none_option/kb_ref/policy is a safe silent default the BOM resolves (policy stays code, but it is NAMED here and every resolved default carries its reason in the response). Kind must_resolve means NO safe default exists — a caller must settle it (agent: ask the user) before the BOM is orderable.

port_model._comment: v0.4.0 unified port model (encoding B). Replaces the former multigig_*_port_count, access_*_fiber, and uplink_* axes with ONE parametrised axis 'port_count' plus the selector enums below. Port capability is DATA: a model carries model.ports (role=access) and each network module carries ports (role=uplink); a configured variant's port pool = model.ports + the fitted module's ports. Subsumption is the per-port speed SET (a 25G SFP28 port lists [1g,10g,25g]); the solver never infers speed downshift.

port_speed.notes: Ordered low->high. Sub-1G (100M) is intentionally NOT a filter speed (display-only). 'mgig' is no longer a speed: a Multigigabit copper port lists its real speed set, e.g. [1g,2.5g,5g,10g]. v0.9.0 added '50g' (Cisco SFP56, single-lane 50G) between 40g and 100g for C9350 SFP56 uplinks (C9350-NM-8L native; C9350-NM-8Y 4x50G mode). Added '200g' and '400g' for Catalyst 9500X QSFP-DD uplinks (C9500X-28C8D/60L4D) — additive legal-value growth only, no registry_version bump, same convention as the 'series'/'stacking_technology' additions.

variables.name: model_id
variables.notes: The exact orderable base SKU (model.id). An open enum: legal values are the model ids present in the loaded KBs, not enumerated here. Pinning it turns a solve into an exact-model lookup — the response's per-candidate domains then serve as the datasheet summary (uplink options, PSUs, licenses, cables). Not reported in open_variables (its open domain IS the candidate list).

variables.name: series
variables.notes: Series is an ordinary variable, not a privileged root. Sub-families are distinct series values. Legal_values will grow as families are added. Added 'MS130' and 'MS150' (native Meraki cloud-managed switches, DB/switching/MS/) without a registry_version bump, since no other axis KIND/ROLE/shape changed. Added 'C9500' and 'C9500X' (Cisco Catalyst 9500 Series, DB/switching/C9500/) on the same precedent: C9500X is a distinct Silicon One Q200-based platform from the UADP-based C9500, mirroring the existing C9300/C9300X split. Added 'C9550' (Cisco C9550 Series Smart Switches, DB/switching/C9550/) — fixed core/agg switches on Silicon One E100/E104, same precedent (legal-value growth only).

variables.name: total_port_count
variables.notes: Physical ACCESS port count. Equals the sum of model.ports[role=access] group counts (validator-checked). Uplinks are NOT included (they come from the fitted module).

variables.name: port_count
variables.notes: PARAMETRISED. A query is {variable:'port_count', where:{role?,medium?,speed}, condition, value}. Resolves against a variant's port pool (model.ports access groups + the fitted module's uplink groups). port_count = number of ports in matching groups whose speeds[] include the requested speed; omitted facets are wildcards — role/medium are OPTIONAL refinements, and the role-agnostic form ('N ports able to run speed S', any role) is the default ask. Multiple port_count constraints at different speeds are checked TOGETHER for pool feasibility (shared physical ports), not independently. 'medium:fiber == 0' excludes fiber-access models.

variables.name: uplink_modular
variables.notes: true: uplinks come from a network module (configurables.network_modules group; the fitted member contributes role=uplink ports to a variant). false: fixed uplinks, carried inline in model.ports as role=uplink rows.

variables.name: uplink_module
variables.notes: The fitted uplink network module — the configured variant the solver searches over. An open enum: legal values are the module SKUs in the model's referenced network-module group. Pinning it eliminates models whose group lacks that module (and pins the variant); leaving it open lets a port demand select it. DEFAULT RULING (recorded, revisitable): a standalone switch ships with NO module (group none_option); a port demand that requires a module overrides this with the first feasible option. Whether an agent-facing default should instead be 'cheapest module' or must_resolve is an open per-variable ruling this field exists to carry.

variables.name: poe_capable
variables.notes: Discriminating, not monotonic: a non-PoE model is a deliberate (cheaper) choice, so both true and false are meaningful filters. Derivable from poe_type != none; kept explicit for the schema's PoE present-ness conditionals.

variables.name: poe_type
variables.notes: ORDERED level (none<poe<poe+<upoe<upoe+). '>=' means 'this level or better' (a UPOE switch satisfies a poe+ request by downshift); '==' means 'exactly this level' (cost-conscious, don't upsell). level_watts is the per-port standard (the SINGLE SOURCE for the UI/MCP PoE-demand translation: a 'N ports at level L' demand contributes N x level_watts[L] to poe_budget_watts, and max(level) to a poe_type>= constraint). Not a model field.

variables.name: poe_budget_watts
variables.notes: Present only when poe_capable is true. Value = max achievable across the switch's poe_budget_matrix (the PSU-pair delivering a requested wattage is resolved from that matrix at solve time). A UI may gather this as ports x per-level watts instead of a raw total.

variables.name: stacking_capable
variables.notes: Monotonic: presence never hurts. Sensible queries are 'require' (==true) or 'don't care' (omit); '==false' is rarely meaningful and the UI should treat 'not required' as omit, not as ==false.

variables.name: stacking_technology
variables.notes: Required and non-blank on a model when stacking_capable is true. May be left unconstrained in a QUERY. v0.10.0 added 'stackwise-160' (C9200 modular, 160 Gbps data stacking) and 'stackwise-80' (C9200L fixed, 80 Gbps). Added 'meraki-stack-100g' with explicit human sign-off for MS150 (native Meraki hardware stacking via 2x dedicated 100G stacking ports / MA-CBL-100G-* cables, 80 Gbps stacking bandwidth — not a Cisco StackWise technology, DB/switching/MS/). Added 'stackwise-virtual' with explicit human sign-off for C9500 (StackWise Virtual, SVL): a licensed, software-defined stack formed over ordinary front-panel uplink ports, not a dedicated physical stack cable — Advantage-tier only (Network Essentials does not include it, per datasheet Table 24 'High availability and resiliency'). C9500 models therefore carry NO configurables.stack_cables / stack_cable_group; the capability is realized entirely via this enum value plus the Advantage license, not an orderable cable SKU. No registry_version bump (legal_values grew only, same convention as the 'series' addition).

variables.name: stack_cable
variables.notes: The stack cable fitted in the kitlist. An open enum: legal values are the model's stack-cable group members. Never eliminates a model — a pick only refines the BOM. Default: the group's none_option (a standalone switch needs no cable); when stacking_capable is a hard requirement the named policy substitutes the shortest cable. Absent entirely on models without a stack_cables configurable (e.g. C9500 StackWise Virtual — see stacking_technology notes).

variables.name: stackpower_cable
variables.notes: Same pattern as stack_cable, for StackPower. Never eliminates; default none_option, shortest when stackpower_capable is required.

variables.name: psu_config
variables.notes: The resolved PSU set (primary [, secondary [, tertiary]]). NOT directly constrainable (accepted_conditions is empty) — it is steered indirectly through poe_budget_watts, psu_redundancy, and psu_triple, and resolved by the named 'psu-default' policy (code, with every choice carrying a reason in the response): ship the model's default_primary; ADD a secondary to meet a higher PoE load; upsize the primary only when no secondary covers it; redundancy forces a matched pair; triple forces a tertiary-capable row. Declared here so the PSU choice is an honest, visible part of the residual decision space (its domain = the model's valid_primary + poe_budget_matrix rows).

variables.name: psu_redundancy
variables.notes: Off by default: the kitlist ships a single PSU (configurables.power_supplies.default_primary), and a secondary is added only when a PoE request exceeds what the default primary alone delivers. When ON, the configurator forces a redundant (primary, secondary) pair. Never eliminates a model.

variables.name: psu_triple
variables.notes: Off by default. When ON, forces a triple-PSU (primary+secondary+tertiary) configuration on models whose poe_budget_matrix has a tertiary-capable row; has no effect on models without one (e.g. two-bay chassis). Never eliminates a model.

variables.name: license_regime
variables.notes: Set of regimes a model OFFERS (enum-set). First-class user-selectable input. Couples to switch selection as an ordinary hard constraint (e.g. meraki-subscription -> -M models only). MUST_RESOLVE: there is no safe silent default — the right regime depends on customer context (existing dashboard, existing estate) the tool cannot infer, and a wrong guess produces a confidently wrong quote. A caller (agent) must settle it before the BOM is final. License TERM is its own configuration variable resolved at the kitlist stage.

variables.name: license_tier
variables.notes: Set of tiers a model OFFERS (enum-set), derived from its license groups: DNA -E -> [essentials], -A -> [advantage], Meraki -M -> [essentials, advantage]. A FILTER on offered-tier capability (parallel to license_regime): choosing 'advantage' drops -E hardware, keeps -A and -M. The essentials-vs-advantage PICK on a model that offers both (Meraki -M) is still a config choice the BOM resolves; this variable only removes models that cannot provide the chosen tier. MUST_RESOLVE: tier determines the license SKU and feature set — no safe silent default. Stored on the model (mirrors license_regime); validator checks it equals the union of the model's license-group tiers.

variables.name: license_term
variables.notes: Subscription term length in years. legal_values is the universe; the per-model domain is the license group's choices_years (some groups carry no term_variable — the pick then resolves to the group's single device-tied SKU, flagged not_applicable). The pick resolves the concrete subscription SKU (matched on the '-<n>Y' suffix). Never eliminates a model. MUST_RESOLVE: an orderable subscription needs a term; there is no safe default length.
